input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle used for ACC studies, retrofitted as a teleoperated platform with advanced sensors and control technology. The physical twin comprises a lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements designed to support real‑time ACC algorithms and safety‑critical performance evaluation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers three key services: 1) Data Monitoring – a user interface displays real‑time vehicle state data along with projected driving paths and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save and generate reports; 2) Computation Offloading – the DT runs the ACC algorithm in a cloud or better‑suited computing environment, calculating desired speeds based on lead‑vehicle distances and returning commands to the vehicle for actuation; 3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, or trigger emergency braking remotely via the UI. The services cater to operators (real‑time monitoring and control) and developers (data collection, debugging, and performance analysis).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system requires a shared notion of time across all vehicle devices to enable computation offloading. The design mandates clock synchronization better than 100 ms—specifically a 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations—to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular application standards. Temporal granularity for digital‑physical data exchanges is therefore bounded by this 100 ms synchronization window.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system implements a single Digital Twin instance distributed over three logical entities—Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User—each mapped onto separate hardware components within the vehicle. The architecture is decentralized, with each entity encapsulating distinct responsibilities (data pre‑processing, actuation, user interface) and coordinating via ROS 2 middleware. No separate twin instances are created, thus the multiplicity is one, while coordination mechanisms rely on Fe‑based node communication profiles.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture; key software components identified in Table I are mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) and deployed as individual ROS 2 packages, each containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The User Entity hosts a User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel. The Digital Twin Entity bundles data‑management, control‑mechanisms, and Data Storage (which lacks a natural FE). The Physical Twin Entity encompasses sensor/actuator interfaces, processing such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and device communication. This entity‑based decomposition across separate hardware nodes enables fine‑grained resource management and system‑wide coordination, though it deviates from ISO 23247 by exposing a Physical Twin FE.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the vehicle’s LiDAR, motor, steering and servo actuators, and vehicle state are streamed in real‑time from the physical twin to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi network. The raw LiDAR point cloud is transmitted as a series of point coordinates via UDP, while vehicle state metrics are carried in ROS2 messages. Prior to consumption, the Data Translation component serializes all streams, aligns message types, and applies unit conversions. Preprocessing is performed by a dedicated Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, which partitions the cloud into object tracks and projected driving paths. Data quality assurance is provided by type alignment and unit normalisation within the Data Translation layer. Event triggers are simply the arrival of new sensor packets; updates to the 3D visualization are performed continuously, achieving continuous low‑latency real‑time display.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: High‑level control commands such as target speed and steering angle are published by the Digital Twin control logic on ROS 2 topics. The Actuation FE receives these publications, performs data translation, and translates the desired speed into a motor voltage command and the steering setpoint into a servo position command. These actuator‑level commands are conveyed to the vehicle’s motor controller and servo driver, which execute the physical actuation. The Data Translation FE ensures inbound commands are correctly typed and unit‑converted. The system does not specify explicit authentication, conflict‑resolution, or feedback confirmation mechanisms in the provided documentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Digital Twin software components communicate internally using ROS2 middleware (DDS). Inter‑entity communication between the vehicle (physical twin) and the user interface (digital twin) is performed over a Wi‑Fi wireless network using UDP. The network topology includes an edge node inside the vehicle hosting the data translation, point‑cloud processing and visualization services, and a client node in the user’s cockpit. Bandwidth requirements are dictated by the high‑throughput LiDAR point‑cloud stream; latency must remain sub‑millisecond to maintain real‑time visualization. No explicit reliability, redundancy or security mechanisms are documented.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process follows ISO 23247 Part 2’s entity‑based reference architecture. A Digital Twin is composed of three core entities—Data Collection & Device Control, Digital Twin, and User—each of which is structured from functional entities responsible for specific tasks such as OME actuation, data conversion to human‑interpretable formats, and user interaction. The architecture is intentionally open‑ended, permitting the implementation of functional entities using any suitable technology; a common choice highlighted is OPC Unified Architecture to mediate communication between physical devices and the digital representation. While the document does not explicitly describe version control, quality assurance, or testing procedures, these are implicitly managed through the modular, entity‑centric decomposition described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: ISO 23247 ‑ Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (Part 2: Reference Architecture) compliance is established. Interoperability is facilitated by optional use of OPC Unified Architecture for device‑to‑digital communication. No further certification or compliance framework details are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system implements Adaptive Cruise Control following ISO 15622‑2018 performance requirements and test procedures, and its V2X communication latency adheres to the constraints defined in 3GPP TS 22.185. No additional cybersecurity or safety protocol details are provided in the source.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

C++... MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

The mention of 'single Digital Twin instance' is consistent with the scope of the case study. Supported by quotes: 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity', 'SW components that implement data management... are organized into the Digital Twin Entity', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'.

This is a highly specific and technically grounded description of the architecture. It correctly maps components to ISO 23247 Functional Entities (FEs), describes the deployment across nodes (ROS2), and notes the specific deviation from the standard regarding the Physical Twin Entity. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA', and 'By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (DTS), comprising the User, Digital, and Physical Twin entities. While it mentions general concepts like 'cloud-based fleet coordination' in the abstract, it does not describe specific integration patterns, data exchange protocols, or interoperability with external systems or other Digital Twins within the case study implementation. Therefore, the absence is correctly identified.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It also lists the 'ACC' model which 'Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed', and the 'Data Storage' component which 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes physical actuators and control mechanisms. Section III.B explicitly lists a 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and describes the 'lower level chassis' which is 'responsible for controlling the steering and the speed'. Table I lists the 'Actuation' component which 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', and Section IV.A R5 describes the ability to 'perform emergency braking' via a user interface.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', yet the source text details the sensing infrastructure. Section III.B specifies the 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. Table I also lists the 'Data Collection' component which 'Collects object tracks and ego vehicle state'.

While the document mentions 'high-fidelity' as a general definition of Digital Twins and establishes system requirements like latency (R7) and clock synchronization (R8), it does not provide specific details on model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods for the computational models, uncertainty quantification, or error detection and correction mechanisms. The focus is on architectural fitness and functional requirements rather than model validation. Quote: 'Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' (general definition only).

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and grounded in the source text. It accurately identifies the data sources (LiDAR, motor, steering), the protocols (Wi-Fi, UDP, ROS2), and the specific processing steps (Data Translation serialization, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). The text supports these details: 'Streams of data from LiDAR... are fused in real time', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP', and 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. The extraction correctly interprets the technical implementation described in the case study.

The description accurately captures the control flow from the digital twin to the physical vehicle. It correctly states that high-level commands (target speed, steering angle) are published on ROS2 topics and received by the Actuation FE, which translates them into motor voltage and servo commands. This is directly supported by Table I: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.' It also correctly identifies the role of the Data Translation FE for validation. The note about the lack of explicit authentication or conflict-resolution mechanisms is accurate, as the text does not mention them.

The extraction provides a correct and specific overview of the network infrastructure based on the text. It accurately notes the use of 'ROS2 middleware' for internal communication and 'Wi-Fi wireless network using UDP' for inter-entity links. The description of the topology (edge node in vehicle, client node in cockpit) aligns with the architecture diagram and descriptions. The latency requirement is supported by R7: 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity'. The statement regarding the lack of explicit reliability/security mechanisms is consistent with the provided text, which focuses on functional requirements rather than network security protocols.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions specific hardware used (e.g., 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano') and general deployment locations (vehicle vs. user's cockpit), but it does not provide the detailed deployment infrastructure characteristics requested, such as 'hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise)' distinctions beyond the physical hardware, specific 'computational and storage requirements' (e.g., CPU/RAM specs), 'scalability mechanisms', or 'availability/reliability assurance' protocols. Since the document lacks these specific architectural details regarding hosting, the 'Not in Document' assessment is accurate.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on the implementation of a specific case study for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Digital Twin (DT) using ISO 23247. While the text mentions using DTs 'During the design phase of automotive systems' and 'During vehicle operation', it does not describe specific lifecycle *stages* of the DT itself (e.g., development, deployment, maintenance, decommissioning), representation types at different phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. The context is a specific engineering project rather than a lifecycle management framework. Therefore, the claim that this characteristic is not in the document is accurate.

The description accurately captures the engineering process based on ISO 23247 Part 2's entity-based reference architecture. It correctly identifies the core entities: 'Data Collection and Device Control Entity', 'Digital Twin Entity', and 'User Entity', and their composition from 'Functional Entities' responsible for tasks like 'actuating the OME, transforming data into a human-interpretable format, or interfacing with the user'. It correctly notes the open-ended nature of the architecture, stating 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented', and highlights 'OPC Unified Architecture' as a common choice. The description also correctly assesses the absence of explicit version control or testing procedures in the text. The extraction is highly specific, detailed, and strongly grounded in the provided sources.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents describe user interaction capabilities (e.g., 'view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data', 'enabling/disabling ACC') and the ACC system's logic ('determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed'). However, the text does not describe the Digital Twin's decision support capabilities in terms of analytics, processing capabilities for generating insights beyond basic control logic, confidence measures for recommendations, or specific decision-making processes that support the user. The focus is on the system's functionality and architecture rather than analytical insights. Therefore, the characteristic is indeed not present in the document.

The description correctly identifies compliance with 'ISO 23247 - Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (Part 2: Reference Architecture)'. This is supported by the source text: 'Currently, the only DT-specific architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247... Part 2 of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA'. It also accurately notes the interoperability facilitated by 'OPC Unified Architecture', supported by the quote: 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'. The conclusion that no further certification details are provided aligns with the text, which focuses on the architecture rather than certification frameworks. The extraction is specific, detailed, and well-supported.
